nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still blackwater streams, forest pools, and flooded areas with dense vegetation, leaf litter, and dark tannin-stained water

decorPreferences: heavy planting with floating plants to diffuse light
dark substrate (fine sand or dark gravel)
driftwood and leaf litter to tannin-stain the water

diet.notes: Micropredator. Accepts micro-flake, crushed dried foods, and frozen/live small invertebrates — baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Moina, Daphnia, and grindal worms are all taken readily. Foods must be appropriately small to match the fish's mouth size.

goodWith: corydoras and other small bottom-dwelling catfish
small tetras and other peaceful characids
other pencilfish species (when kept in separate schools)
avoidWith: large or boisterous tankmates that may outcompete or intimidate
dwarf shrimp (cherry shrimp, neocaridina) — tend to be predated

summary: One of the most popular pencilfish in the hobby, Beckford's Pencilfish is a slender, cigar-shaped schooling characin from Amazonian blackwaters. Peaceful and best kept in groups of 6+, it favors the upper water column and pairs well with corydoras and small tetras.
careNotes:
Beckford's Pencilfish is one of the most widely kept pencilfish and is generally considered manageable for aquarists with some experience keeping soft-water species. It prefers slightly acidic, soft blackwater conditions — adding Indian almond (catappa) leaves or driftwood to tannin-stain the water is commonly recommended and tends to reduce stress and bring out more vivid coloration. A tight-fitting lid is advisable, as the species has been known to jump when startled, though it is not considered a high-risk escape artist.

This species is a top-zone dweller, spending most of its time near or just below the surface. It pairs naturally with bottom-dwelling corydoras and mid-water tetras, creating a well-distributed community layout without direct competition for space. Groups of 6 or more are strongly recommended — smaller numbers can result in increased skittishness and uneven aggression between males.

Males display noticeably enhanced red ventral coloration during courtship and dominance displays, and will spar with one another in the typical pencilfish manner (facing off head-to-head or parallel). This behavior is normal and rarely results in injury when group size is adequate and the tank is sufficiently planted.

The species exhibits a diurnal color rhythm — a vertical dark barring pattern appears at night that fades by day. This is a natural and well-documented behavior, even documented in blind specimens, and is not a disease indicator.

Avoid housing with cherry shrimp or other small neocaridina — they are likely to be treated as food. Crystal shrimp and larger Amano shrimp also face some risk. Large sulawesi shrimp (Caridina spinata) are generally safer if kept together.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer with no parental care; adults will readily consume eggs and fry, so removal of adults (or use of a dense moss/mop spawning setup with mesh) is recommended for successful fry yield. Breeding is most reliably triggered at the upper end of the temperature range (~26-27°C) with soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0-6.5) and good aeration. Eggs typically hatch within 1-2 days; fry become free-swimming around 4-6 days post-hatching. First foods for fry are infusoria, Paramecium, or very fine powdered dry foods (5-50 micron range); baby brine shrimp can be introduced once fry are large enough. The species tends to spawn readily in mature, well-planted aquariums even without deliberate intervention.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary source. Original Aquarium Co-Op URL returned HTTP 404 — SeriouslyFish substituted as primary per playbook swap rule.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Pencilfish care guide covering N. beckfordi specifically. Confirmed temperature (24-27°C), pH (6.5-7.5), min group size (6), schooling requirement, swim zone (midwater-upper), diet (baby brine, daphnia, crushed flake), and compatibility. Notes escape risk and tight lid recommendation. Tank size 20gal (76L) confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Characiformes / Lebiasinidae), max size 6.5cm SL, native range (Guyana south to Amazon, up to Rio Negro), temperature 24-26°C, pH 6.0-8.0. FishBase max size (6.5cm) is larger than most trade sources; adult size set to 3.0-5.0cm reflecting typical captive/trade size rather than the recorded maximum SL.

additional.name: Tropical Fish and Aquariums
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed tank size (20gal / ~76L), temperature range (21-27°C), pH (6.0-7.5), adult size (3.8-5.1cm), lifespan (~5 years), diet, and egg-scattering breeding behavior. Swim zone described as mid-level with angled swimming posture.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; adults eat their own eggs. Separate adults post-spawning or use fine mesh/dense moss. Fry require infusoria or powdered fry food initially.
